app.title: The Hidden Carbon Cost of Deforestation
app.thesis: Reported forest emissions are only half the story — the lost future carbon sink is the rest.
app.footer: Data: World Bank Open Data · EDGAR (EN.GHG.*). A portfolio piece.

baseline.label: Baseline
baseline.menuLabel: Forgone carbon sink computed from year:

unit.label: Unit
unit.carLong: annual emissions of a passenger car

methodology.title: Methodology
methodology.body: Reported (official) emissions are the World Bank deforestation figures. Full accounting adds the forgone carbon sink — the future sequestration lost when forest is cleared — estimated as an R factor times cumulative area loss. R combines an aboveground rate with a locked allometric factor of 1.24; the conservative / mid / high band reflects its uncertainty. Estimates are dashed; measured values are solid. Composite figures start in 2000 (stock coverage) while the sink integral runs from 1990.

intro.heading: The half of the story we don't count
intro.p1: When a forest is cleared, the carbon it stored is reported as an emission. That reported number is real — but it is only the visible half.
intro.p2: The other half is the sink we lose: the carbon that forest would have kept absorbing for decades. This piece makes that hidden cost visible. Read it as an "at least this much" argument.

main.heading: The reported number
main.p1: This is deforestation as it is officially counted: the carbon stock released when forest is cleared, tracked over time. Explore it by horizon, region and baseline — but hold onto the question of what it leaves out.

mainSink.heading: The hidden cost
mainSink.p1: Now add the forgone sink — the future sequestration lost with the forest. It draws on top of the reported stock, and the full picture is several times larger. The ×N badge shows how much the reported figure understates it.

crossing.heading: When the loss overtakes the release
crossing.p1: The stock release is a one-off impulse; the forgone sink keeps accumulating year after year. Run the projection far enough and the rising sink loss overtakes the one-time release.

footprint.heading: Deforestation in context
footprint.p1: Against fossil emissions, deforestation is the smaller share — but it is far from negligible once the forgone sink is counted alongside the released stock.

insight.heading: Zoom into deforestation
insight.p1: Remove fossil and the deforestation footprint fills the frame: the released stock in green, the forgone sink in amber. The same grammar as the main chart — the reported number, and the cost we don't count.
insight.caption: Looking at the absolute numbers, and with fossil emissions set aside, deforestation stops looking so insignificant…

baselineLab.caption: See what happens when you manipulate with the baseline year — drag it back toward 1800 and watch the forgone sink grow.

method.heading: How is the forgone carbon sink actually computed?
method.p1: The forgone sink is a simple product: a sequestration rate R times the area of forest lost each year. R is just how much CO₂ a hectare of that forest would keep pulling out of the air every year if it were still standing.
method.p2: R is not one universal number. It differs from forest to forest, because it depends on the balance between two opposing flows: the CO₂ a living forest absorbs as it grows, and the CO₂ it releases as wood dies and decomposes.
method.p3: The R values we use are traceable gross-flux measurements — the net sink is the difference between those two flows, absorbed minus released. The bigger that gap, the more sink each cleared hectare quietly costs us.

amplified.caption: For these very reasons, some climate models suggest the forgone sink could be up to six times larger. What would that mean in practice? Slide the R multiplier and watch.
